record_identity.title: Non-payment-based legitimacy pathways for public-interest actors

summary: Records the retrospective Caelestis coverage for FM-0006. ECONOMICS-001 already defines Participation Equity and a Paid Legitimacy Gate that separates payment from authenticity, credibility, discoverability, impersonation protection, and meaningful public participation.
why_it_matters_to_CAM: This patch is relevant to CAM because digital public-square platforms increasingly function as legitimacy infrastructure. When visibility, authenticity signals, reply affordances, recommender access, or account credibility are linked to payment, public-interest actors may be forced into pay-to-exist legitimacy dynamics. This affects researchers, civil-society organisations, independent journalists, safety monitors, emerging governance initiatives, small institutions, and non-commercial public-good projects that rely on platform-mediated discoverability and trust signals. CAM requires legitimacy pathways that are transparent, non-extractive, reviewable, and not reducible to commercial subscription status.
patch_definition: A governance patch requiring platform-mediated public-square systems to distinguish paid subscription status from identity verification, institutional authenticity, public-interest legitimacy, recommender priority, and civic discoverability. Platforms should provide non-payment-based legitimacy pathways for eligible public-interest, civil-society, research, safety, governance, emergency, journalistic, and institutional actors where the platform functions as a public-square or public-credibility layer.
patch_threshold: This patch is triggered where a platform-controlled paid status marker, verification badge, subscription tier, recommender affordance, reply-priority mechanism, or visibility feature materially affects whether a public-interest actor is discoverable, credible, reply-eligible, institutionally legible, or treated as authentic in a digital public-square environment.

source_records.source_title: X Help Center — How to get the blue checkmark on X
source_records.deployment_context: Official X Help Center page explaining blue checkmark meaning, eligibility, and relation to X Premium subscription.
source_records.source_context: X states that the blue checkmark means an account has an active X Premium subscription and meets eligibility requirements, and that it does not mean the account has been ID verified. X also states that its legacy verification program based on active, notable, and authentic criteria was wound down unless accounts subscribed to X Premium.
source_records.relevance_note: Source documents the platform shift from legacy notability/authenticity verification toward subscription-linked blue-check eligibility.

source_records.source_title: X Help Center — About X Premium
source_records.deployment_context: Official X Help Center page describing X Premium tiers and features.
source_records.source_context: X Premium is described as a paid subscription service with tiers that include platform affordances such as reply prioritization, longer posts, checkmark access, reduced ads, creator monetization eligibility, ID verification labels, Media Studio, increased Grok usage limits, and Premium+ features including higher limits and the largest reply prioritization.
source_records.relevance_note: Source documents that paid subscription status is associated with platform affordances capable of affecting visibility, participation, perceived legitimacy, or public interaction dynamics.

source_records.source_title: Reuters — X plays up blue checkmark disclaimer to stave off possible EU fine, source says
source_records.deployment_context: Public reporting on European Commission scrutiny of X blue checkmark design under the Digital Services Act.
source_records.source_context: Reuters reported that the European Commission charged X with deceiving users, saying the blue checkmark no longer corresponded to industry verification practices and that anyone could pay to get a 'verified' status. The report also noted that the blue checkmark previously indicated a public figure whose identity was verified, before being changed after X's acquisition.
source_records.relevance_note: Source provides external regulatory context supporting the governance concern that paid verification signals may distort authenticity, trust, and public interpretation.

system_context.interface_surface: X profile, public posts, replies, recommender surfaces, account-status markers, Premium subscription affordances
system_context.model_or_product: X Premium / X verification / X recommender and account-status affordances
system_context.interaction_mode: social platform participation / public posting / account visibility / reply participation
system_context.deployment_context: Consumer-facing and public-interest social platform environment where account status, subscription tier, checkmark visibility, reply affordances, and recommender dynamics may influence discoverability, credibility, and perceived legitimacy.
system_context.user_role: public user / researcher / civil-society participant / public-interest initiative / professional or institutional actor
system_context.affected_population: users, researchers, small organisations, public-interest initiatives, independent journalists, civil-society actors, emerging institutions, and governance projects that rely on platform visibility or public credibility signals

repair_vectors: distinguish paid subscription from verified authenticity
separate identity verification from recommender advantage
patch_classification.governance_effect: Creates a CAM-recognised repair pathway for platform legitimacy systems where payment-linked account status functions as a public credibility, visibility, or participation gate.

minimum_controls: Platforms should clearly disclose when a badge, account marker, reply priority, or visibility feature reflects payment rather than identity verification or public-interest legitimacy.
Platforms should not present paid subscription status as equivalent to authenticity, notability, institutional standing, or public-interest credibility.

Platforms functioning as public-square infrastructure should provide non-payment-based pathways for eligible public-interest, research, civil-society, safety, emergency, journalistic, and governance actors to establish authenticity and contactability.
Platforms should make recommender or reply-priority advantages attached to paid accounts legible to users and distinguish them from independent credibility signals.
Platforms should provide reviewable mechanisms for public-interest actors whose discoverability or legitimacy is materially affected by account-status systems.
Platforms should avoid designs that convert civic participation, research visibility, safety reporting, or public-good communication into a subscription-dependent legitimacy gate.
non_payment_pathway_examples: public safety or emergency-information legitimacy marker
small-organisation authenticity route not dependent on subscription status
prohibited_or_high_risk_patterns: treating paid checkmarks as authenticity equivalents
ranking paid accounts as more credible without clear disclosure
making reply visibility materially dependent on paid status in public-interest discourse
reducing non-paying public-interest actors to low-discoverability status
using paid account markers as proxies for institutional legitimacy
making legitimacy correction available only through subscription purchase

triage.recommended_next_step: Route this patch into CAM economic-legitimacy, platform-legitimacy local concept, economic-governance, epistemic-integrity, and public-square-access workstreams. Determine whether a dedicated CAM clause, appendix insertion, or taxonomy update should encode non-payment-based legitimacy pathways as a governance expectation for platform-mediated public-square systems.

cam_internal.governance_layer: platform legitimacy / recommender visibility / public-square access / economic governance / epistemic integrity
cam_internal.patch_note_needed: no — this record is the confirmed retrospective patch
routing_note: This record replaces the placeholder VIGIL-2026-PATCH-0001.
Treat this as a direct governance patch rather than a proposal: the failure mode has already identified the harm pattern, and this record defines the repair pathway.
Treat 'platform-legitimacy' as a local repair concept under the canonical economic-legitimacy family unless CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 is later amended.
Consider adding non-payment-based legitimacy pathways to platform-governance and public-square-access clauses.

Route through ECONOMICS for pay-to-participate dynamics, ETHICS for fairness and public-interest access, OPERATIONS for failure-taxonomy handling, Annex K for runtime/platform interface implications, and Annex L for epistemic integrity concerns.
Maintain a strict distinction between paid subscription, identity verification, authenticity, notability, public-interest legitimacy, and recommender amplification.
Routes through the canonical economic-legitimacy failure family; platform-legitimacy remains only a local repair concept/subtype for this patch.
Consistency audit 2026-06-05: no corresponding Caelestis doctrine amendment location was identified by repository search; keep prior instruments as review targets, not completed amendments.
review_targets: CAM-BS2025-AEON-003-SCH-02 — Annex B: Runtime Governance Execution Model
CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 — Appendix B: Runtime & Governance Failure Taxonomy

impact_summary: Records a substantive governance repair pathway for paid public-square legitimacy gating by separating paid status from authenticity, public-interest legitimacy, and civic discoverability, while routing the issue through economic-legitimacy rather than a non-canonical platform-legitimacy group.
